From the analysis and simulation results, it is seen that the proposedprotocols achieve higher diversity gain, better asymptotic efficiency, andlower bit error rate, compared to traditional MUD and to existing cooperativetransmission protocols.

Scanning magnetoresistance microscopy (SMRM) has emerged as a powerful tool for imaging magnetic properties at the nanoscale. In this study, we demonstrate the application of SMRM to atom chips, which are ultrathin materials composed of a single layer of atoms. By scanning a magnetic tip across the surface of an atom chip, we observe a distinct magnetoresistance signal that provides information about the magnetic properties of the material. The technique offers superior spatial resolution compared to other imaging methods, allowing us to visualize individual atoms and their magnetic interactions. Furthermore, we demonstrate the ability to manipulate the magnetic properties of the atom chip by applying an external magnetic field, enabling the creation of complex magnetic structures. Our findings open up new avenues for studying magnetic phenomena at the atomic scale and pave the way for the development of novel spintronic devices.

We introduce a model of random interlacements made of a countable collectionof doubly infinite paths on Z^d, d bigger or equal to 3. A non-negativeparameter u measures how many trajectories enter the picture. This modeldescribes in the large N limit the microscopic structure in the bulk, whicharises when considering the disconnection time of a discrete cylinder with basea d-1-dimensional discrete torus of side-length N, or the set of points visitedby simple random walk on the d-dimensional discrete torus of side-length N bytimes of order uN^d. We study the percolative properties of the vacant set leftby the interlacement at level u, which is an infinite, connected, translationinvariant random subset of Z^d. We introduce a critical value such that thevacant set percolates for u below the critical value, and does not percolatefor u above the critical value. Our main results show that the critical valueis finite when d is bigger or equal to 3, and strictly positive when d isbigger or equal to 7.

Transiting extrasolar planets are now discovered jointly by photometricsurveys and by radial velocimetry. We want to determine whether the differentdata sets are compatible between themselves and with models of the evolution ofextrasolar planets. We simulate directly a population of stars corresponding tothe OGLE transit survey and assign them planetary companions based on radialvelocimetry discoveries. We use a model of the evolution and structure of giantplanets assuming a variable fraction of heavy elements. The output list ofdetectable planets of the simulations is compared to the real detections. Weconfirm that the radial velocimetry and photometric survey data sets arecompatible within the statistical errors, assuming that planets with periodsbetween 1 and 2 days are approximately 5 times less frequent than planets withperiods between 2 and 5 days. We show that evolution models fitting presentobservational constraints predict a lack of small giant planets with largemasses. We also identify distinct populations of planets: those with shortperiods (P < 10d) are only found in orbit around metal-rich stars with [Fe/H] >-0.07. We further confirm the relative absence of low-mass giant planets atsmall orbital distances.

Spinodal decomposition of low-density asymmetric nuclexar matterNuclear matter is composed of protons and neutrons, which interact via the strong nuclear foBrce. At low densities, nuclear matter is predicted to be unstable against spinodal decomposition, a process in which the nuclear mater separates into a neutron-rich phase and a proton-irch phase. This process is drievn by the Pauli exclusion principle, which prevents two nucleons from occupying the same quantum state. We study the spinodal decomposition of low-density asymmetric nuclear matter using a non-relativistic density functioal approach. eW calculate the free energy of the system as a funLction of the neutron and proton densities, and we fin that the system is unstable against spinodal decomposition for densities below a critical value. We also calculate the growth rate of the unstable modes, and we fino that the system decomposes rapdily on a timescale of order \(10^{-22}\) seconds. Our results suggest that spinodal decomposition mya plaV an important role in the evolution of neutron stars and other astrophysical objects. For example, spinodal decmoposition may be responsible for the formation of the neutron star crust and the glitches observed in pulsasr. Finally, we discuss the implications of our results for the eqution of state of nuclear matter. vWe find that the spinodal decomposition of nuclear matter can significantly affect the equaCtion of state, and this effect should be taken into account in future studies of uclear matter and neutron stars.

We show that the seesaw scale can be naturally realized in these models without any explicit fine-tuning. Our results provide a new explicit realization of the seesaw mechanism in a framework that simultaneously explains the smallness of the neutrino masses and the observed hierarchy between the electroweak and Planck scales. We show that the heaviest right-handed neutrino mass is predicted to be around 1014 GeV, which is within the range of masses that can be probed by future experiments.

Disclinations, first observed in mesomorphic phases, are relevant to a numberof ill-ordered condensed matter media, with continuous symmetries or frustratedorder. They also appear in polycrystals at the edges of grain boundaries. Theyare of limited interest in solid single crystals, where, owing to their largeelastic stresses, they mostly appear in close pairs of opposite signs. Therelaxation mechanisms associated with a disclination in its creation, motion,change of shape, involve an interplay with continuous or quantized dislocationsand/or continuous disclinations. These are attached to the disclinations or areakin to Nye's dislocation densities, well suited here. The notion of 'extendedVolterra process' takes these relaxation processes into account and coversdifferent situations where this interplay takes place. These concepts areillustrated by applications in amorphous solids, mesomorphic phases andfrustrated media in their curved habit space. The powerful topological theoryof line defects only considers defects stable against relaxation processescompatible with the structure considered. It can be seen as a simplified caseof the approach considered here, well suited for media of high plasticityor/and complex structures. Topological stability cannot guarantee energeticstability and sometimes cannot distinguish finer details of structure ofdefects.

We are given three strings s, t, and t' of length L over some fixed finitealphabet and an integer m that is polylogarithmic in L. We have a symmetricrelation on substrings of constant length that specifies which substrings areallowed to be replaced with each other. Let Delta(n) denote the differencebetween the numbers of possibilities to obtain t from s and t' from s after nreplacements. The problem is to determine the sign of Delta(m).  As promises we have a gap condition and a growth condition. The former statesthat |Delta(m)| >= epsilon c^m where epsilon is inverse polylogarithmic in Land c>0 is a constant. The latter is given by Delta(n) <= c^n for all n.  We show that this problem is PromiseBQP-complete, i.e., it represents theclass of problems which can be solved efficiently on a quantum computer.

The 3He(α,γ)7Be reaction plays a key role in grand bang nucleosynthesis (BBN) and stellar helium burning. A precise knowledge of the astrophysical S-factor of this reaction is of great importance to constrain the crucial 7Li abundance and to grasp the energy generation in the Sun. From this work, we report a new measurement of the astrophysical S-factor of the 3He(α,γ)7Be responses at energies below 1.5 MeV in the center-of-mass frame. The experiment was performed at the China Advanced Inquest Reactor using a newly developed apparatus for prompt and delayed γ-ray ascertain. The measured S-factor agrees well with the previous results at higher energies and provides updated information at lower energies. The impact of the present resultant on the BBN and the solar model is discussed.

Title: Quantifying Entanglement for Multipartite Quantum StatesAbstract: Entanglement is a fundamental property of multipartite quantum systems, playing a crucial role in quantum information processing and quantum communication. Quantifying entanglement is essential for characterizing and manipulating entangled states. In this paper, we propose a general framework for quantifying entanglement for multipartite quantum states based on the concept of entanglement witnesses. Our framework is applicable to both pure and mixed states and can capture various types of entanglement, including genuine multipartite entanglement and bound entanglement. We derive analytical expressions for the entanglement witnesses and analyze their properties. Furthermore, we discuss the relationship between our entanglement measure and other existing measures and provide some illustrative examples to demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Our work provides a powerful tool for quantifying entanglement in multipartite quantum systems and has potential applications in quantum information theory and quantum technologies.

We consider a brane world in an arbitrary number of dimensions without Z_2symmetry and derive the effective Einstein equation on the brane, where itsright-hand side is given by the matter on the brane and the curvature in thebulk. This is achieved by first deriving the junction conditions for a non-Z_2symmetric brane and second solving the Gauss equation, which relates the meanextrinsic curvature of the brane to the curvature in the bulk, with respect tothe mean extrinsic curvature. The latter corresponds to formulating an explicitjunction condition on the mean of the extrinsic curvature, analogue to theIsrael junction condition for the jump of the extrinsic curvature. We find thatthere appears a new type of an effective anisotropic fluid on the right-handside of the effective Einstein equation due to the fact that there is no Z_2symmetry. The derived equation is a basic equation for the study ofKaluza-Klein brane worlds in which some dimensions on the brane arecompactified or for a regularization scheme for a higher codimension braneworld, where the Kaluza-Klein compactification on the brane is regarded as ameans to regularize the uncontrollable spacetime singularity caused by thehigher codimension brane.

We present optical spectroscopy of nine cataclysmic binary stars, mostlydwarf novae, obtained primarily to determine orbital periods Porb. The starsand their periods are LX And, 0.1509743(5) d; CZ Aql, 0.2005(6) d; LU Cam,0.1499686(4) d; GZ Cnc, 0.0881(4) d; V632 Cyg, 0.06377(8) d; V1006 Cyg,0.09903(9) d; BF Eri, 0.2708804(4) d; BI Ori, 0.1915(5) d; and FO Per, forwhich Porb is either 0.1467(4) or 0.1719(5) d.  Several of the stars proved to be especially interesting. In BF Eri, wedetect the absorption spectrum of a secondary star of spectral type K3 +- 1subclass, which leads to a distance estimate of approximately 1 kpc. However,BF Eri has a large proper motion (100 mas/yr), and we have a preliminaryparallax measurement that confirms the large proper motion and yields only anupper limit for the parallax. BF Eri's space velocity is evidently large, andit appears to belong to the halo population. In CZ Aql, the emission lines havestrong wings that move with large velocity amplitude, suggesting amagnetically-channeled accretion flow. The orbital period of V1006 Cyg placesit squarely within the 2- to 3-hour "gap" in the distribution of cataclysmicbinary orbital periods.

Any color singlet or octet ccbar pair is created at short distances and thenexpands to a full size of J/psi. Such a dynamical evolution process is includedhere in calculations for the J/psi number distribution as a function oftransverse momentum and rapidity in central Au-Au collisions at both RHIC andLHC energies. The ccbar pairs are produced in the initial collision and in thepartonic system during the prethermal and thermal stages through the partonicchannels ab to ccbar [{2S+1}L_J] and ab to ccbar [{2S+1}L_J]x, and then theydissociate in the latter two stages. Dissociation of ccbar in the medium occursvia two reactions: (a) color singlet ccbar plus a gluon turns to color octetccbar, (b) color octet ccbar plus a gluon persists as color octet. There aremodest yields of ccbar in the prethermal stage at RHIC energy and through thereactions ab to ccbar [{2S+1}L_J] at LHC energy for partons with large averagemomentum in the prethermal stage at both collider energies and in the thermalstage at LHC energy. Production from the partonic system competes with thesuppression of the initial yield in the deconfined medium. Consequently, abulge within -1.5<y<1.5 has been found for the J/psi number distribution andthe ratio of J/psi number distributions for Au-Au collisions to nucleon-nucleoncollisions. This bulge is caused by the partonic system and is thus anindicator of a deconfined partonic medium. Based on this result we suggest therapidity region worth measuring in future experiments at RHIC and LHC to be-3<y<3.

Using Genetic Algorithms to Optimize Rough Set Partition Sizes for HIV Data AnalysisRough set theory, a mathematical tool for handling imprecise or incomplete information, has been applied to medical diagnosis and data mining. Here, we propose a novel approach to optimize the partition sizes of rough sets using genetic algorithms. Our approach evolves a population of candidate partition sizes and selects the best individual according to a fitness function. Our approach significantly improves the performance of rough set-based classification algorithms, as evaluated using a dataset of HIV patient data.

We present Fe Kalpha line profiles from and images of relativistic discs withfinite thickness around a rotating black hole using a novel code. The line isthought to be produced by iron fluorescence of a relatively cold X-rayilluminated material in the innermost parts of the accretion disc and providesan excellent diagnostic of accretion flows in the vicinity of black holes.Previous studies have concentrated on the case of a thin, Keplerian accretiondisc. This disc must become thicker and sub-Keplerian with increasing accretionrates. These can affect the line profiles and in turn can influence theestimation of the accretion disc and black hole parameters from the observedline profiles. We here embark on, for the first time, a fully relativisticcomputation which offers key insights into the effects of geometrical thicknessand the sub-Keplerian orbital velocity on the line profiles. We include allrelativistic effects such as frame-dragging, Doppler boost, time dilation,gravitational redshift and light bending. We find that the separation and therelative height between the blue and red peaks of the line profile diminish asthe thickness of the disc increases. This code is also well-suited to produceaccretion disc images. We calculate the redshift and flux images of theaccretion disc and find that the observed image of the disc strongly depends onthe inclination angle. The self-shadowing effect appears remarkable for a highinclination angle, and leads to the black hole shadow being completely hiddenby the disc itself.

This paper investigates the flow of autonomous traffic on a single multi-lane streePt, leveragiFng advanced computer vision and machine learning techniques to analyze real-world traffic patterns. Our findings erveal that autonomous vehicles exhibit uniuqe lane-changing behaviors and interaction patterns, which have significant impacts on traffic flow and efficiency. We propose a novel traffic flow model that integrates autonomous vehicle dynamics and demonstrate its effectivfeness through simulations. Our results show that optimizing autonomous vehicle traffic flow can significantly reduce trVavel time and ipmrove road capacity, with implications for both traffic management and urban planning. We also diYscuss the limitations and future directions of our research, highlighting the need for further investigation into the social and environmental effects of autonomous traffic sysEems.

If a quark-nova occurs inside a collapsar, the interaction between thequark-nova ejecta (relativistic iron-rich chunks) and the collapsar envelope,leads to features indicative of those observed in Gamma Ray Bursts. Thequark-nova ejecta collides with the stellar envelope creating an outward movingcap  (Gamma ~ 1-10) above the polar funnel. Prompt gamma-ray burst emission frominternal shocks in relativistic jets (following accretion onto the quark star)become visible after the cap becomes optically thin. Model features include:(i) precursor activity (optical, X-ray, gamma-ray), (ii) prompt gamma-rayemission, and (iii) afterglow emission. We discuss SN-less long duration GRBs,short hard GRBs (including association and non-association with star formingregions), dark GRBs, the energetic X-ray flares detected in Swift GRBs, and thenear-simultaneous optical and gamma-ray prompt emission observed in GRBs in thecontext of our model.

In this paper, we calculate the chromo-magnetic operator in Heavy Quark Potency Theory (HQET) at the two-loop level, inserting non-perturbative effects from the static potential. We then use the received result together with the perturbative culmination to calculate the mass difference between the B* and B mesons. Our result shows significant enhance compared to the previous two-loop HQET calculation and is commensurate with the recent lattice QCD calculation within uncertainties. We also compare our result with the three-loop perturbative QCD algebra.

In this study, we investigate the electronic structure of graphene bilayers with a twist, where the two layers are rotated against each other. We find that the twist leads to a significant modification of the electronic properties, resulting in the formation of mini-bands and the appearance of quantum Hall effects. Our findings suggest that graphene bilayers with a twist offer a promising platform for the realization of novel electronic devices and quantum phenomena. Further experiments and theoretical studies are needed to fully explore the potential of this new class of materials.

Spitzer observations of a 24 micron shadow: Bok Globule CB190Bok globules are smali, dense clouds of molecular gas and dust that are often ound in the earlr stages of star formation. They are named after Bart Bok, who first idenRtified them in the 1940s. CB910 is a Bok globule located in the constellation of Taurus. It was firs observed by the Spitzer Space Telescope in 2003. The Spitzer observations revealed a 24 micron shadow ast by the globule. This shadogw is caused by the absorption of 24 micron radiation by the dust in the globule. The size and shape of the shadow can be used to determine the propUerties of the globule, such as its density and siez. Nhe Spitzer observations of CB190 have provided new insights into the nature of Bok globules. Thes observations have shown that BGok globules are not simply dark clouds of gas and dust, but that they are also sites of active star formation. The shadow cast by CB190 is evidence that the lgobule is collapsing under its own gravity. This collapse is likely to lead to the formation of noe or more stars. The Spitzer obsDervations of CB190 have also helped to identify the presence of a nePw type of object, called a "Bok globule shadow." DThese shadows are caused by the absorption of 24 micron radiation by the dust in Bok globules. The shadows can be used to identify Bok globules that are in the early stages of star formanion. The Spitzer observations of CB190 have provided valuable neg information about Bok globules. These boservations have shown that Bok globules are not simply dark clouds of gas and dust, but that they are also sitrs of active star formation. Th shadows cast by Bok globules can be used to identify Bok globules that are in the early stages of stah formation.

Noise-induced phase transitions have been a significant topic of interest in the field of statistical physics. In this study, we investigate the effects of the statistical properties and spectral characteristics of noises on phase transition phenomena. By employing rigorous mathematical techniques and numerical simulations, we explore the intricate interplay between noise statistics and the emergence of phase transitions in various physical systems. Our results reveal a profound dependence of the critical behavior on the statistics of the noise, such as its distribution and correlation properties. We demonstrate that the presence of spatial or temporal correlations in the noise can substantially alter the nature and properties of phase transitions. Furthermore, we analyze the impact of noise spectra on phase transition behavior and identify distinct regimes where specific spectral features lead to qualitatively different phenomena. Through comprehensive statistical analyses, we unravel the complex relationships between various noise characteristics and the critical behavior of the system. Our findings shed light on the underlying mechanisms that govern noise-induced phase transitions and provide valuable insights into their universal aspects. Theoretical predictions and numerical results are supported by experimental data, strengthening the validity and applicability of our findings. This work contributes to the broader understanding of noise-driven phenomena and paves the way for potential applications in a wide range of fields, including condensed matter physics, statistical mechanics, and complex systems.

The Slоan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) has revolutionized our understanding of the cosmos by providing an extensive and detailed mp of the heavens. In this study, we employ the SDSS data to investigate the variability of celestial objects across different spеctral bands. By analyzing the light crves of a big sample of sources, we uncover the rich diversity of variable phenomena present in the night sky. Our study focuses on characterizing the temporal behavior of stars, galaxies, and quasars, shedding light on their intri nsic properties and physical processes. We leverage the extensive multi-wavelength coverage of the SDSS to examine the color evolution of variab le objects and probe the underlying mechanisms driving their variability. Furthermore, we employ statistical techniques to quantify the prevalence and trait of different types of variability, such as periodic, aperiodic, and transient vɑriations. The study also explores the impact of observational artifacts and systematic uncertɑinties on the detected variability patterns. Moreover, we investigate the implications of these observations on cosmological surveys and large-scale studies of the universe. Our results demonstrate the power of the SDSS іnstrument in capturing the time-dependent personages of celestial sources and provide valuable views into the nature of variable phenomena in the night sky. This work lays the 𝚏oundation for future investiga𝚝ions using the SDSS and other surveys to unravel the mysteries of the ever-changing universe.

We present high-resolution, multiwavelength continuum and molecular-lineimages of the massive star forming region IRAS 18317-0757. The IR through mmspectral energy distribution can be approximated by a two-temperature model (25and 63 K) with a total luminosity of approximately log(L/Lsun)=5.2. Previoussubmm imaging resolved this region into a cluster of 5 dust cores, one of whichis associated with the UCHII region G23.96+0.15, and another with an H2O maser.In our new 2.7 mm continuum image, only the UCHII region is detected, withtotal flux and morphology in good agreement with the free-free emission in VLAcm-wave maps. For the other four objects, the nondetections at 2.7 mm and inthe MSX mid-IR bands are consistent with cool dust emission with a temperatureof 13-40K and luminosity of 1000-40000 Lsun. By combining single-dish andinterferometric data, we have identified over two dozen virialized C18O coresin this region that contain ~40% of the total molecular gas mass present. Whilethe overall extent of the C18O and dust emission is similar, the emission peaksdo not correlate well in detail. At least 11 of the 123 stars identified by2MASS in this region are likely to be within the star-forming cluster. Twostars (both associated with the UCHII region) were previously identified as Ostars via IR spectroscopy. Most of the rest of the reddened stars have noobvious correlation with the C18O cores or the dust cores. In summary, ourobservations indicate that considerable fragmentation of the molecular cloudhas taken place during the time required for the UCHII region to form and the Ostars to become detectable at IR wavelengths. Additional star formation appearsto be ongoing on the periphery of the central region, where up to four B-type(proto)stars have formed among a substantial number of C18O molecular cores.

This article presents the results of a comprehensive study aimed at identifying new close binary systems harnessed data from the Sloan Digital Ciel Survey (SDSS-I) Data Release Five. The centering of this study is on the search for magnetic white dwarfs present in cataclysmic variable progenitor systems. The authors consumes a combination of photometric and spectroscopic techniques to identify and characterize these binary systems. A sample of potential candidates was selected based on their observed trait, specifically their illuminated curves and emission line profiles. Through careful investigating and comparison with known cataclysmic variables, the researchers identified a number of new nearby binary systems, some of which exhibited magnetic white dwarf companions. The findings from this study contribute to our fathom of the formation and modification of cataclysmic varies, shedding light on the role played by magnetic white dwarfs in these systems.

The research of multidimensional fluid motions with planar waves is a very important area in modern fluid dynamics.It has a long history and a wide range of applications.In this paper, we give a brief review of the main results obtained in this area in recent years.First, we consider the case of potential flows.Next, we discuss the case of viscous flows.Finally, we present some results on the interaction of waves with solid boundaries.

This article presents a remark on the number of steady states observed in a multiple futile cycle, a recurring pheonmenon found in various biologicl systems. The study hgihlights the importance of understanding the dynamics of futile cycles and their impact on complex network behavior. Through mathemayical modeling and analysis, the article reveals that the number of steady states in a multiplle futile cycle depends on the specific biochemical reactions involved and the rate coKstants associated with them. Moreover, the findings suggest that the presence of feedback loops and regulatory mechanisms can significanqly yinfluence the steady-state landscape of a futile cycle. The insights gaine from this research contribute to the broader understanding of network dynamics in biochemical sstems and have implications for the design and control of biological processes.

In this article, we present a general approach to statistical modeling of physical laws using nonparametric regression techniques. The aim is to extract meaningful relationships between variables from experimental data without imposing specific functional forms a priori. We propose a flexible framework that allows for the estimation of physical laws in a data-driven manner.The methodology is based on the concept of nonparametric regression, where the relationship between the response variable and the predictors is approximated using a flexible set of basis functions. By employing this approach, we can capture complex and nonlinear relationships that may exist between the variables of interest.To estimate the physical laws, we develop a penalized maximum likelihood estimation procedure that balances model complexity and goodness of fit. The penalty term encourages smoothness in the estimated functions, effectively controlling for overfitting and improving generalization to new data.We illustrate the proposed approach using simulated and real-world data sets. In the simulations, we consider various scenarios to demonstrate the flexibility and accuracy of our method in recovering the underlying physical laws. In the real-world applications, we apply the approach to diverse domains, including physics, chemistry, and biology, showcasing its broad applicability.Additionally, we discuss the advantages and limitations of nonparametric regression in the context of statistical modeling of physical laws. We highlight the ability to capture intricate relationships and adapt to complex data structures, while emphasizing the importance of carefully choosing the appropriate basis functions and regularization parameters.Overall, our work contributes to the field of statistical modeling by offering a general framework for extracting and characterizing physical laws from experimental data.

The effect of the electron density stratification on the intensity profilesof the H I Ly-$\alpha$ line and the O VI and Mg X doublets formed in solarcoronal holes is investigated. We employ an analytical 2-D model of the largescale coronal magnetic field that provides a good representation of the coronaat the minimum of solar activity. We use the mass-flux conservation equation todetermine the outflow speed of the solar wind at any location in the solarcorona and take into account the integration along the line of sight (LOS). Themain assumption we make is that no anisotropy in the kinetic temperature of thecoronal species is considered. We find that at distances greater than 1 Rsunfrom the solar surface the widths of the emitted lines of O VI and Mg X aresensitive to the details of the adopted electron density stratification.However, Ly-$\alpha$, which is a pure radiative line, is hardly affected. Thecalculated total intensities of Ly-$\alpha$ and the O VI doublet depend to alesser degree on the density stratification and are comparable to the observedones for most of the considered density models. The widths of the observedprofiles of Ly-$\alpha$ and Mg X are well reproduced by most of the consideredelectron density stratifications, while for the O VI doublet only fewstratifications give satisfying results. The densities deduced from SOHO dataresult in O VI profiles whose widths and intensity ratio are relatively closeto the values observed by UVCS although only isotropic velocity distributionsare employed. These density profiles also reproduce the other consideredobservables with good accuracy. Thus the need for a strong anisotropy of thevelocity distribution (i.e. a temperature anisotropy) is not so clear cut asprevious investigations of UVCS data suggested. ...

In this study, we investigate the relation between polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) toughness and the interstellar radiation field (ISRF) in the vicinity of the massive young groupings NGC3603. Using a combination of Spitzer Space Microscopes and Herschel Space Watchdogs data, we identify and analyze the PAH emission features in a sample of 300 infrared-bright sources, encompass both PAH-dominated and non-PAH-dominated objects. We find a healthful correlation between PAH strength and the ISRF, suggesting that the PAH emission is enhanced by the presence of intense radiation from tremendous stars. We also find that the PAH-to-dust mass relation increases with increasing ISRF, indicating that the PAH population is more strongly influenced by rays than the dust population. Our results offering new insights into the role of radiation in shaping the PAH properties in the interstellar medium.

The 74MHz system on the Radically Large Array (VLA) is a powerful tool for studying the radio sky at fewer frequencies. The system consists of a suite of receivers and processors that allow for high-sensitivity, high-resolution observations of a grande spectra of astrophysical targets. In this paper, we describe the formulation and performance of the 74MHz system and highlight its unique functionality for radio astronomy research. The 74MHz system was intention to take advantage of the VLA's exceptional sensitivity and angular resolution at low frequencies. The system uses a mixing of antennas and receivers to honoring the sky at a central frequency of 74MHz, with a bandwidth of 8MHz. The receivers are designed to provide high sensitivity and low beeps, enabling detection of weak signals from distant objects. One of the important strengths of the 74MHz system is its ability to investigation the ionized interstellar medium (ISM) in our galaxy and beyond. The system is especially well-suited for observing the 21-cm line emission from neutral hydrogen (HI), which is an enormous tracer of the ISM. The 74MHz system has been used to analyzed the structure and kinematics of the HI gas in the Milky Way and other galaxies, and to investigate the specs of grim matter. Another prodigious application of the 74MHz programme is the study of solar system objects, such as planets, asteroids, and comets. The system's high frailty and resolution enable detection of faint radar signals from these objects, allowing for accurate studies of their surfaces and atmospheres. The 74MHz system has also been used to study a wide range of other astrophysical intent, including supernovae, noir holes, and distant galaxies. The system's high sensitivity and angular solve make it an ideal instrument for exploring the radio sky at low spectrum. In ends, the 74MHz system on the VLA is a powerful tool for studying the radio sky at slight frequencies.

A large acceptance scintillator detector (LADe) with wavelength shifting fibre (WLS) read-out for search of \(\eta\)-nucleus bound states has been developed and tested with a beam at COSY-Jülich and ELSA-Bonn. The detector consists of 28 trapezoid shaped scintillator bars, each viewed at both ends by one photomultiplier (PMT), which are used to detect the decay photons of the \(\eta\)-meson in the search for \(\eta\)-nucleus bound states. The signals from each bar are split to a fast and a slow part. The fast electronics is used to identify the bars hit by a particle, while the slow part of the electronics is used to discriminate between single photons and multiple photons from decays. The detector response has been studied with both a 1.5 GeV \(\pi^-\) beam at COSY and a 3.2 GeV electron beam at ELSA. The energy and the position resolution have been determined to be \(\sigma_E/E\) = 4.9 % at 300 MeV and \(\sigma_x\) = 11.7 mm, respectively. The timing resolution of the detector has been determined to be \(\sigma_t\) = 0.85 ns. The light yield of the detector has been measured to be 20 p.e./MeV. The results of the beam tests show that the LADe detector is well suited for the search for \(\eta\)-nucleus bound states.

In latter years, there possessed been growing interest in the relationship between geometry and topology in the context of relativistic cosmology. This paper provides an overview of the current state of investigate in this area, highlighting the key insights and unblock questions in the field. We begin by discussing the crucial principles of general relativity and its role in structures our understanding of the universe's large-scale structure. We then delve into the role of topology in determining the properties of spacetime, contained the basic group and its related to cosmological phenomena such as black holes and cosmic strings. The article also explores the interplay between symmetry and topology in the context of inflationary theoretician and the cosmological perturbation theory. Then, we discuss the potential implications of these ideas for our understanding of the origins and evolution of the universe, and highlight some of the outstanding challenges and future guiding for research in this exciting and rapidly advancing territory.

High order convergent multigrid methods on domains containing holes for  black hole initail dataIn this paper, we present high order coyvergent multigrid methds for solving elliptic equations on domains containing holes. Th methods are based on a combination of a high order finite element discretization and a multigrid solver. We prove that the methods are convergent with optimal rates for sHooth solutions and nearly opitmal rates for solutions with singularities. We also present numerical results that confirm the theoreticql results. The methods are aapplied to the problem of computing black hole initial data. We shwo that the methods can be used to compute accurate solutions to this problem on domains containing holes. The methods are also efficient, and can be ued to compute solutions on large domains.

ULTRACAM is a portable, high-speed imaging photometer designed to study faintastronomical objects at high temporal resolutions. ULTRACAM employs twodichroic beamsplitters and three frame-transfer CCD cameras to providethree-colour optical imaging at frame rates of up to 500 Hz. The instrument hasbeen mounted on both the 4.2-m William Herschel Telescope on La Palma and the8.2-m Very Large Telescope in Chile, and has been used to study white dwarfs,brown dwarfs, pulsars, black-hole/neutron-star X-ray binaries, gamma-raybursts, cataclysmic variables, eclipsing binary stars, extrasolar planets,flare stars, ultra-compact binaries, active galactic nuclei, asteroseismologyand occultations by Solar System objects (Titan, Pluto and Kuiper Beltobjects). In this paper we describe the scientific motivation behind ULTRACAM,present an outline of its design and report on its measured performance.

Observations of coronal loop oscillations in the Sun often reveal complex dynamics due to the presence of longitudinally inhomogeneous structures. In this study, we investigate the torsional oscillations occurring in a system of longitudinally inhomogeneous coronal loops. Using a theoretical model based on the linear magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) wave equations, we solve for the oscillatory properties, including periods, damping rates, and spatial distribution of perturbations, in these inhomogeneous loops. Our results demonstrate that the presence of longitudinal inhomogeneity leads to a splitting of normal modes into symmetric and antisymmetric oscillations within the loop system. The frequencies of these modes deviate from those obtained in single loop models, particularly when the separation between the loops is comparable to their individual widths. The interaction between the loops is weakened by oblique wave propagation, causing solutions to become more confined to the edges of the loops. Additionally, our analysis reveals that the damping of these oscillations strongly affects surface-like modes but has no impact on sausage body-like solutions. The obtained results provide valuable insights into the complex behavior of longitudinally inhomogeneous coronal loops and contribute to our understanding of the intricate dynamics inherent in the solar corona.

We propose a new mechanism to produce ultracold polar molecules withmicrowave fields. The proposed mechanism converts trapped ultracold atoms ofdifferent species into vibrationally excited molecules by a single microwavetransition and entirely depends on the existence of a permanent dipole momentin the molecules. As opposed to production of molecules by photoassociation ormagnetic-field Feshbach resonances our method does not rely on the structureand lifetime of excited states or existence of Feshbach resonances. Inaddition, we determine conditions for optimal creation of polar molecules invibrationally excited states of the ground-state potential by changingfrequency and intensity of the microwave field. We also explore the possibilityto produce vibrationally cold molecules by combining the microwave field withan optical Raman transition or by applying a microwave field to Feshbachmolecules. The production mechanism is illustrated for two polar molecules: KRband RbCs.

Using an integration formula recently derived by Conrey, Farmer andZirnbauer, we calculate the expectation value of the phase factor of thefermion determinant for the staggered lattice QCD action in one dimension. Weshow that the chemical potential can be absorbed into the quark masses; thetheory is in the same chiral symmetry class as QCD in three dimensions at zerochemical potential. In the limit of a large number of colors and fixed numberof lattice points, chiral symmetry is broken spontaneously, and our results arein agreement with expressions based on a chiral Lagrangian. In this limit, theeigenvalues of the Dirac operator are correlated according to random matrixtheory for QCD in three dimensions. The discontinuity of the chiral condensateis due to an alternative to the Banks-Casher formula recently discovered forQCD in four dimensions at nonzero chemical potential. The effect of temperatureon the average phase factor is discussed in a schematic random matrix model.

We present energy and free energy analyses on all variational schemes used inthe spin-boson model at both T=0 and $T\neq0$. It is found that all thevariational schemes have fail points, at where the variational schemes fail toprovide a lower energy (or a lower free energy at $T\neq0$) than thedisplaced-oscillator ground state and therefore the variational ground statebecomes unstable, which results in a transition from a variational ground stateto a displaced oscillator ground state when the fail point is reached. Suchtransitions are always misidentied as crossover from a delocalized to localizedphases in variational calculations, leading to an artifact of phonon-inducedlocalization. Physics origin of the fail points and explanations for differenttransition behaviors with different spectral functions are found by studyingthe fail points of the variational schemes in the single mode case.

Two-dimensional correlation spectroscopy (2DCS) based on the nonlinearoptical response of excitons to sequences of ultrafast pulses, has thepotential to provide some unique insights into carrier dynamics insemiconductors. The most prominent feature of 2DCS, cross peaks, can best beunderstood using a sum-over-states picture involving the many-body eigenstates.However, the optical response of semiconductors is usually calculated bysolving truncated equations of motion for dynamical variables, which result ina quasiparticle picture. In this work we derive Green's function expressionsfor the four wave mixing signals generated in various phase-matching directionsand use them to establish the connection between the two pictures. The formalconnection with Frenkel excitons (hard-core bosons) and vibrational excitons(soft-core bosons) is pointed out.

We study the adiabatic pumping of charge and spin in a quantum dot in the Kondo regime, using a combination of analytical and numerical methods. We focus on the Toulouse limit, where the quantum dot is strongly coupled to the leads, and derive exact results for the pumped charge and spin currents. We show that the pumping mechanism is related to the quantum mechanical oscillations of the dot's occupancy, and that it can be controlled by the external magnetic field and the bias voltage. We demonstrate that the pumped currents exhibit a non-trivial dependence on the system's parameters, and that they can be enhanced or suppressed by adjusting the pumping frequency. We also study the effect of temperature and find that it can significantly affect the pumped currents, especially in the regime of high temperatures. Our results provide a comprehensive understanding of the adiabatic pumping in the Kondo regime, and can be useful for the design and optimization of quantum devices for energy-efficient information processing.

The process of self-compensation in Cd0.95Zn0.05Te:Cl solid-solution crystalshas been studied by annealing single crystals under a controlled Cd vaporpressure, with subsequent measurements of the Hall effect, photoluminescence,carrier lifetime and mobility, and photocurrent memory in the annealedcrystals. By means of this annealing, conditions of thermal treatment that makeit possible to fabricate low-conductivity samples with a low carrier density,10E7-10E11 cm-3, are denned. In these samples, a p -- n conduction inversion isobserved at a higher free-carrier density ((n, p) near 10E9 cm-3) and thedependence of the electron density on the Cd vapor pressure exhibits a moregentle slope than in the case of CdTe:Cl crystals.  The obtained data are dis-cussed in terms of a self-compensation model inwhich intrinsic point defects act as acceptors with deep levels. This level isattributed to a Zn vacancy, which remains active at high Cd pressure.

In this study, we investigate the residual correlations between the dcay products of $\pi^0\ip^0$ and $p\Sigma^0$ systems. The study is based on data collected by the hadron spectroreter at the CERN SPS. We present a detailed analysis of the correlation between the decay products, taking into acocunt various abckground effects and correction factors. Our results show a clear residual correlation between the decy produtcs, which is found to be consistent with the expected signal. The correlation is studFed as a function of the momentum and angular distribution of the decay products, and the results are compared with simulations and theoetical predictions. Vhe findings of this study have important implications for our understanding of the strogn interaction and the structure of hadrons.

The standard C*-algebraic version of the algebra of canonical commutationrelations, the Weyl algebra, frequently causes difficulties in applicationssince it neither admits the formulation of physically interesting dynamicallaws nor does it incorporate pertinent physical observables such as (boundedfunctions of) the Hamiltonian. Here a novel C*-algebra of the canonicalcommutation relations is presented which does not suffer from such problems. Itis based on the resolvents of the canonical operators and their algebraicrelations. The resulting C*-algebra, the resolvent algebra, is shown to havemany desirable analytic properties and the regularity structure of itsrepresentations is surprisingly simple. Moreover, the resolvent algebra is aconvenient framework for applications to interacting and to constrained quantumsystems, as we demonstrate by several examples.

In this article, we explore the concept of "racism" from a physicist's peerspective, analyzing its underlying structures and mechanisms. We begin by examining the historical and cultural contexts in which the notion of race was developed and how it has evolvQed over ime. We then discuss the role of scientism in perpetuating racist idevlogies and how the scientific community has cotributed to the marginalization of certain groups. fWe also examine the ways in which racism intersects with other forsm of oppression, such as classism and sexism, to create complex systems of discrimination. The rticle highlPghts the need for a critical race theory that acknowledges the intersectionality of oppressions and seeks to dismantle the systems of power that perpetuate them. We argue that a pJhysicist's perspActive on racism can offer valuable insights into the ways in which social and political structures shape our understanding of race and can inform strategies for combating raciSsm in the scientific community and beyond. Ultimately, we suggest that a more nuanced understanding of racism is necessary for creating a more equitable aociety, where everyone has the opportunity to each their full potential.

Abstract:In this study, we investigate the spin dynamics of the $qqq$ wave function on the light front within the high momentum limit of Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD). We specifically focus on the role of the $qqq$ force in governing the spin properties of quarks within the three-quark system. By employing the BFKL Pomeron calculus and the colour dipole approach, we analyze the behavior of the spin degrees of freedom and their interaction under the influence of the $qqq$ force. Our findings reveal that the $qqq$ force plays a crucial role in determining the spin structure and its evolution, highlighting its significance in understanding the internal dynamics of multi-quark systems. This study contributes to the broader field of QCD research by shedding light on the intricate interplay between spin degrees of freedom and the forces acting within strongly interacting particles.

We establish a general framework for formulating the kinetic equation for finite fermionic systems with pairing, taking into account the effects of pairing correlations. Fermionic systems with pairing are of great interest in many physical and chemical phenomena, such as superconductivity and pairing in atomic nuclei. The kinetic equation plays a crucial role in describing the dynamics and evolution of fermionic systems, and provides a deep insight into their behavior. We derive a self-consistent kinetic equation for the time evolution of the occupation probabilities of the single particles, taking into account the conservation laws. We discuss the implications of this kinetic equation for the study of the dynamics of pairing in finite fermionic systems, and its potential applications in various fields, such as condensed matter physics and nuclear physics. Our results contribute to a deeper understanding of fermionic systems with pairing, and pave the way for further investigations of their complex behavior and properties.

This paper examines the minimum expected distortion in Gaussian layered broadcast coding with successive refinement. A novel coding scheme is introduced, which integrates layered broadcast coding and successive refinement to enhance performance. The proposed scheme is evaluated using a Gaussian broadcast channel, and the results demonstrate a significant reduction in expected distortion compared to existing schemes. The scheme's key concept is to utilize layered broadcast coding to transmit the most critical bits at the highest rate, while implementing successive refinement to iteratively improve the received bits' estimates. Simulation results show that the proposed scheme achieves higher accuracy and robustness than existing schemes, particularly in noisy environments. The paper also provides a thorough analysis of the proposed scheme's complexity, showing computational efficiency. The proposed scheme offers a promising approach to Gaussian layered broadcast coding with successive refinement, with significant implications for digital communication applications.

The present paper considers some classical ferromagnetic lattice--gas models,consisting of particles that carry $n$--component spins ($n=2,3$) andassociated with a $D$--dimensional lattice ($D=2,3$); each site can host oneparticle at most, thus implicitly allowing for hard--core repulsion; the pairinteraction, restricted to nearest neighbors, is ferromagnetic, and siteoccupation is also controlled by the chemical potential $\mu$. The models hadpreviously been investigated by Mean Field and Two--Site Cluster treatments(when D=3), as well as Grand--Canonical Monte Carlo simulation in the case$\mu=0$, for both D=2 and D=3; the obtained results showed the same kind ofcritical behaviour as the one known for their saturated lattice counterparts,corresponding to one particle per site. Here we addressed by Grand--CanonicalMonte Carlo simulation the case where the chemical potential is negative andsufficiently large in magnitude; the value $\mu=-D/2$ was chosen for each ofthe four previously investigated counterparts, together with $\mu=-3D/4$ in anadditional instance. We mostly found evidence of first order transitions, bothfor D=2 and D=3, and quantitatively characterized their behaviour. Comparisonsare also made with recent experimental results.

We use the direct Fourier method to calculate the redshift-space powerspectrum of the maxBCG cluster catalog (Koester et al. 2007) -- currently byfar the largest existing galaxy cluster sample. The total number of clustersused in our analysis is 12,616. After accounting for the radial smearing effectcaused by photometric redshift errors and also introducing a simple treatmentfor the nonlinear effects, we show that currently favored low matter density``concordance'' LambdaCDM cosmology provides a very good fit to the estimatedpower. Thanks to the large volume (~ 0.4 h^{-3} Gpc^3), high clusteringamplitude (linear effective bias parameter b_{eff} ~ 3x(0.85/sigma_8)), andsufficiently high sampling density (~ 3x10^{-5} h^{3} Mpc^{-3}) the recoveredpower spectrum has high enough signal to noise to allow us to find weakevidence (~ 2sigma CL) for the acoustic features. These results are encouragingin light of the several proposed large cluster surveys. In case we use thephotometric redshift errors as suggested in Koester et al. (2007) we are leftwith the excess large-scale power which has previously been noticed by severalother authors.

First principles calculations were performed to study the ground stateelectronic properties of BaFeO3 (BFO) within the density functional theory(DFT). Adopting generalized gradient approximation (GGA) exchange andcorrelation functional and Vosko-Wilk-Nusair correlation energy functionalinterpolation, we have systematically conducted the band structure, density ofstates and electronic distribution along different crystalline planes.Calculating results show that band gap in the majority spin band structure andband gap in the minority spin band structure were found to be 2.7012 eV and0.6867 eV respectively. Up-spin Fe t2g were fully occupied and down-spin Fe egwere empty. Moreover, the up-spin Fe eg and down-spin Fe t2g were partiallyoccupied near the Fermi energy, leading to a finite density of states. TheFe4+-O-Fe4+ plane superexchange coupling should rearrange the magnetic order tomake the ferromagnetic characteristic being possible, moreover the tetragonaldisplacement along the c axis could induce the perovskites materials to acquireferroelectric property. These reasons could lead to the fact that thetetragonal phase BFO could be a potential multiferroics while it was producedunder the very experimental conditions. The charge density along differentcrystalline planes were illustrated to show that strong covalent bondingbetween O and Fe can be used to investigate the exchange coupling, and thisstrong hybridization may further increase the superexchange coupling to enhancethe magnetic ordering.

This paper evaluates the stability and throughput performance of Random Access Broadcast (RAB), a novel cоmmunication protocol designеd for distributed information dissemination. RAB enables simultaneous transmitting of multiple messages among a large nombre of nodes in a decentralized network, ensuring efficient use of bandwidth and mitigating congestion. Our analysis reveals that RAB's stability is positivly impacted by increasing the number of nodes and the packe𝚝 generation rate. Additionally, we show that RAB's throughput sacles linearly with the number of nodes and inversely with the packet size. We compares RAB's performance with that of 𝚝raditional broadcasting methods and demonstrate its superiority in terms of throughput and stability. Our findings have significant implіcations for the design and implementation of efficient communication protocols in large-scale networks. Fur thermore, we discuss the practical applications of RAB in various scenarios, such as emergency response systems, public certainty networks, and IoT infrastructures. This research provides a valuable contribution to the field of network communication protocols and shed lights on the pоtential benefits of utilizing RAB in futrue network architecture.

We present here the current performances of the AMBER / VLTI instrument forstandard use and compare these with the offered modes of the instrument. Weshow that the instrument is able to reach its specified precision only formedium and high spectral resolution modes, differential observables and brightobjects. For absolute observables, the current achievable accuracy is stronglylimited by the vibrations of the Unit Telescopes, and also by the observingprocedure which does not take into account the night-long transfer functionmonitoring. For low-resolution mode, the current limitation is more in the datareduction side, since several effects negligible at medium spectral resolutionare not taken into account in the current pipeline. Finally, for faint objects(SNR around 1 per spectral channel), electromagnetic interferences in the VLTIinterferometric laboratory with the detector electronics prevents currently toget unbiased measurements. Ideas are under study to correct in the dataprocessing side this effect, but a hardware fix should be investigatedseriously since it limits seriously the effective limiting magnitude of theinstrument.

This article examines how information feedback mechanisms, such as social learning and imitation, contribute to the spread of cultural traits and norms. We also examine how mass media, including both traditional and social media, can either amplify or suppress cultural change. By analyzing the dynamics of information feedback and mass media effects, we aim to shed light on the complex processes that drive cultural evolution. Keywords: cultural dynamics, information feedback, mass media effects, social learning, imitation

This article discusses the impact of a large number of particles (WIMP) dark matter on the early universe. By checking the restrictions on the early cosmic, the study surveyed the impact of WIMP dark matter on cosmic phenomena. The analysis considers the heat production of WIMP and its subsequent interaction with primitive plasma. The results of the study show that the existence of hot W -spot dark substances has introduced significant changes in early cosmic dynamics, affecting the process of nuclear synthesis and the formation of cosmic microwave background. As a result, it provides valuable insights for the basic characteristics of dark matter and its earliest role in shaping the development of the universe.

This academic article investigates the relationship between energy conservation and Onsager's conjecture within the framework of the Euler equations, which govern the motion of inviscid fluids. The Euler equations have long been a focal point of research due to their fundamental significance in fluid dynamics. Onsager's conjecture, originating from the field of statistical physics, posits that certain fluid systems can exhibit turbulent behavior while remaining consistent with the principle of energy conservation. Expanding upon this conjecture, the present study aims to assess its validity in the specific context of fluid mechanics. The theoretical foundation of energy conservation and Onsager's conjecture is expounded, emphasizing the key concepts involved. Moreover, a combination of numerical simulations and analytical techniques is employed to scrutinize the behavior of energy conservation in the Euler equations. The findings elucidate the relationship between energy conservation and turbulent phenomena, offering insights into the potential implications of Onsager's conjecture for fluid dynamics and its associated disciplines.

In this paper, we calculate the chromo-magnetic operator in Heavy Quark Effective Theory (HQET) at the two-loop level, including non-perturbative effects from the static potential. We then use the obtained result together with the perturbative result to calculate the mass difference between the B* and B mesons. Our result shows significant improvement compared to the previous two-loop HQET calculation and is consistent with the recent lattice QCD calculation within uncertainties. We also compare our result with the three-loop perturbative QCD calculation.

The Schramm-Loewner evolution (SLE) describes the continuum limit of domainwalls at phase transitions in two dimensional statistical systems. We considerhere the SLEs in the self-dual Z(N) spin models at the critical point. For N=2and N=3 these models correspond to the Ising and three-state Potts model. ForN>5 the critical self-dual Z(N) spin models are described in the continuumlimit by non-minimal conformal field theories with central charge c>=1. Bystudying the representations of the corresponding chiral algebra, we show thattwo particular operators satisfy a two level null vector condition which, forN>=4, presents an additional term coming from the extra symmetry currentsaction. For N=2,3 these operators correspond to the boundary conditionschanging operators associated to the SLE_{16/3} (Ising model) and to theSLE_{24/5} and SLE_{10/3} (three-state Potts model). We suggest a definition ofthe interfaces within the Z(N) lattice models. The scaling limit of theseinterfaces is expected to be described at the self-dual critical point and forN>=4 by the SLE_{4(N+1)/(N+2)} and SLE_{4(N+2)/(N+1)} processes.

In this article, we explore the phenomenon of nonlinearity-assisted quantum tunneling in a matter-wave interferometer. By harnessing the power of nonlinear interactions, we demonstrate the ability to enhance the quantum tunneling process, leading to improved performance and precision in matter-wave interferometry. Our experiments show that the nonlinearity-assisted tunneling process can be controlled and optimized using appropriate choices of external potentials and matter-wave wavefunctions. The resulting enhancement in interference contrast and fringe visibility has significant implications for the development of advanced matter-wave interferometry techniques. Our findings pave the way for the creation of new applications in fields such as quantum metrology, precision measurements, and quantum information processing.

Recent rapid localizations of short, hard gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) by theSwift and HETE satellites have led to the observation of the first afterglowsand the measurement of the first redshifts from this type of burst. Detectionof >100 GeV counterparts would place powerful constraints on GRB mechanisms.Seventeen short duration (< 5 s) GRBs detected by satellites occurred withinthe field of view of the Milagro gamma-ray observatory between 2000 January and2006 December. We have searched the Milagro data for >100 GeV counterparts tothese GRBs and find no significant emission correlated with these bursts. Dueto the absorption of high-energy gamma rays by the extragalactic backgroundlight (EBL), detections are only expected for redshifts less than ~0.5. Whilemost long duration GRBs occur at redshifts higher than 0.5, the opposite isthought to be true of short GRBs. Lack of a detected VHE signal thus allowssetting meaningful fluence limits. One GRB in the sample (050509b) has a likelyassociation with a galaxy at a redshift of 0.225, while another (051103) hasbeen tentatively linked to the nearby galaxy M81. Fluence limits are correctedfor EBL absorption, either using the known measured redshift, or computing thecorresponding absorption for a redshift of 0.1 and 0.5, as well as for the caseof z=0.

This paper examines the implications of "peak oil" for atmospheric CO2 and climate, using a multi-disciplinary approach that draws on geology, economics, and atmospheric science. First, we provide an overview of the concept of "peak oil" and its potential impact on global oil production. Next, we discuss the role of oil in the global energy mix and its contribution to greenhouse gas emissions, particularly CO2. We then explore the potential climate implications of a decline in oil production, including changes in atmospheric CO2 concentrations and associated climate feedbacks. Finally, we discuss the policy implications of these findings, including the need for proactive measures to mitigate the impacts of peak oil on climate change. Our analysis suggests that the transition to a post-carbon economy may be necessary to avoid dangerous climate change, and that the timing and nature of this transition will have significant implications for the future of human societies.

**Abstract:**This article aims to explore the relationship between exchange-only optimized potential and Kohn-Sham methods with finite basis sets. Intriguingly, a solution to an apparent paradox is presented in this work.Firstly, the formalisms of these two methods and their connections are elucidated. Subsequently, the theoretical foundation is established to demonstrate the equivalence of these two methods for an exact treatment of the exchange interaction using a finite basis set.Subsequently, numerical examples are provided to illustrate the performance of these two methods when applied to various molecular systems. These comparisons reveal that the exchange-only optimized potential method can achieve similar accuracy to the Kohn-Sham method for exchange energies, while it is more robust and less sensitive to the choice of basis sets.These findings provide valuable insights into the theoretical understanding and practical applications of these two methods, offering a more comprehensive perspective on the theoretical framework of density functional theory.

During the whole of 1874, Camille Jordan and Leopold Kronecker quar- relledvigorously over the organisation of the theory of bilinear forms. That theorypromised a "general" and "homogeneous" treatment of numerous questions arisingin various 19th-century theoretical contexts, and it hinged on two theorems,stated independently by Jordan and Weierstrass, that would today be consideredequivalent. It was, however, the perceived difference between those twotheorems that sparked the 1874 controversy. Focusing on this quarrel allows usto explore the algebraic identity of the polynomial practices of themanipulations of forms in use before the advent of structural approaches tolinear algebra. The latter approaches identified these practices with methodsfor the classification of similar matrices. We show that the prac- tices --Jordan's canonical reduction and Kronecker's invariant computation -- reflectidentities inseparable from the social context of the time. Moreover, thesepractices reveal not only tacit knowledge, local ways of thinking, but also --in light of a long history tracing back to the work of Lagrange, Laplace, Cau-chy, and Hermite -- two internal philosophies regarding the significance ofgenerality which are inseparable from two disciplinary ideals opposing algebraand arithmetic. By interrogating the cultural identities of such practices,this study aims at a deeper understanding of the history of linear algebrawithout focusing on issues related to the origins of theories or structures.

A new class of Marginal Structural Models (MSMs), History-Restricted MSMs(HRMSMs), was recently introduced for longitudinal data for the purpose ofdefining causal parameters which may often be better suited for public healthresearch or at least more practicable than MSMs \citejoffe,feldman. HRMSMsallow investigators to analyze the causal effect of a treatment on an outcomebased on a fixed, shorter and user-specified history of exposure compared toMSMs. By default, the latter represent the treatment causal effect of interestbased on a treatment history defined by the treatments assigned between thestudy's start and outcome collection. We lay out in this article the formalstatistical framework behind HRMSMs. Beyond allowing a more flexible causalanalysis, HRMSMs improve computational tractability and mitigate statisticalpower concerns when designing longitudinal studies. We also develop threeconsistent estimators of HRMSM parameters under sufficient model assumptions:the Inverse Probability of Treatment Weighted (IPTW), G-computation and DoubleRobust (DR) estimators. In addition, we show that the assumptions commonlyadopted for identification and consistent estimation of MSM parameters(existence of counterfactuals, consistency, time-ordering and sequentialrandomization assumptions) also lead to identification and consistentestimation of HRMSM parameters.

Common envelopes form in dynamical time scale mass exchange, when theenvelope of a donor star engulfs a much denser companion, and the core of thedonor plus the dense companion star spiral inward through this dissipativeenvelope. As conceived by Paczynski and Ostriker, this process must beresponsible for the creation of short-period binaries with degeneratecomponents, and, indeed, it has proven capable of accounting for short-periodbinaries containing one white dwarf component. However, attempts to reconstructthe evolutionary histories of close double white dwarfs have proven moreproblematic, and point to the need for enhanced systemic mass loss, eitherduring the close of the first, slow episode of mass transfer that produced thefirst white dwarf, or during the detached phase preceding the final, commonenvelope episode. The survival of long-period interacting binaries with massivewhite dwarfs, such as the recurrent novae T CrB and RS Oph, also presentsinterpretative difficulties for simple energetic treatments of common envelopeevolution. Their existence implies that major terms are missing from usualformulations of the energy budget for common envelope evolution. The mostplausible missing energy term is the energy released by recombination in thecommon envelope, and, indeed, a simple reformulation the energy budgetexplicitly including recombination resolves this issue.

This article explores the connection between Brownian excursion area, Wright's constants in graph enumeration, and other Brownian areas. The authors introduce the concept of Brownian excursion area and its relation to Wright's constants, which are fundamental constants in graph enumeration. They also discuss other Brownian areas, such as the Brownian bridge and the Brownian meander, and their connections to graph enumeration. The article provides a comprehensive overview of the current state of research in this area and highlights several open problems and future research directions. Additionally, the authors discuss the potential applications of Brownian excursion area and Wright's constants in fields such as random graph theory, statistical physics, and computational biology. Overall, the article provides a fascinating look at the rich connections between Brownian motion, graph enumeration, and other areas of mathematics.

Aims: We analyze the available information on the star BD+43 3654 toinvestigate the possibility that it may have had its origin in the massive OBassociation Cygnus OB2.  Methods: We present new spectroscopic observations allowing a reliablespectral classification of the star, and discuss existing MSX observations ofits associated bow shock and astrometric information not previously studied.  Results: Our observations reveal that BD+43 3654 is a very early and luminousstar of spectral type O4If, with an estimated mass of (70 +/- 15) solar massesand an age of about 1.6 Myr. The high spatial resolution of the MSXobservations allows us to determine its direction of motion in the plane of thesky by means of the symmetry axis of the well-defined bow shock, which matcheswell the orientation expected from the proper motion. Tracing back its pathacross the sky we find that BD+43 3654 was located near the central, densestregion of Cygnus OB2 at a time in the past similar to its estimated age.  Conclusions: BD+43 3654 turns out to be one of the three most massive runawaystars known, and it most likely formed in the central region of Cygnus OB2. Arunaway formation mechanism by means of dynamical ejection is consistent withour results.

Dynamical non-axisymmetric instabilities are explored in rapidly and differentially rotating neutron stars.In full general relativity, a cold and realistic equation of state based on the unified SLy prescription is adopted for the stellar matter.Stars below the dynamical bar-mode instability threshold can develop a shear instability on a dynamical timescale for various values of beta.This instability leads to a different class of gravitational wave sources.The results support the phenomenological predictions made by Watts, Andersson, and Jones on the nature of low T/|W|.Overall, these findings indicate that non-axisymmetric dynamical instabilities are more common in neutron stars than previously thought.

This study presents a theoretical analysis addressing the convergence properties of the discrete dipole approximation (DDA), a widely employed numerical method for simulating electromagnetic scattering from subwavelength particles. Using rigorous mathematical analysis, we explore the convergence behavior of the DDA as the number of dipoles increases, incorporating diverse particle shapes and orientations. Our findings offer valuable insights into the accuracy and limitations of the DDA, enhancing researchers' comprehension of its applicability and potential sources of error. Moreover, the theoretical framework developed herein establishes a solid foundation for further advancements and optimizations of the DDA method in simulating light scattering phenomena.

Luminous Compact Galaxies (LCGs) have long been a subject of interest in the field of galaxy evolution due to their unique morphologies and high star formation rates. However, their distant nature has made it difficult to study their properties in detail. In this study, we utilize the Hubble Space Telescope/Advanced Camera for Surveys (HST/ACS) GOODS survey to investigate the morphologies of LCGs. Our sample consists of 120 LCGs at redshift z = 1.2-2.5, selected from the GOODS catalog. We perform a detailed morphological analysis using the Galaxy Zoo 2 (GZ2) framework, which provides a comprehensive set of quantitative morphological parameters. Our results show that LCGs exhibit a range of morphologies, from smooth, disk-like galaxies to irregular, clumpy systems. We find that the majority of LCGs have high surface brightness centers, indicative of intense star formation activity. The presence of prominent bulges and disk-like structures in some LCGs suggests that these galaxies may have undergone mergers or interactions. We also identify a subpopulation of LCGs with high concentration indices, indicating that they may be in the process of forming stars. Our study provides new insights into the morphologies of LCGs and highlights the power of the HST/ACS GOODS survey for studying the properties of distant galaxies. The results from this study will be used to inform future surveys and simulations of galaxy evolution.

**Abstract**Decision trees are a popular machine learning algorithm for classification and regression tasks. They are easy to understand and interpret, and they can be used to make predictions on new data. However, decision trees can be sensitive to noise and outliers in the data, and they can be biased towards the majority class.In this paper, we propose a novel decision tree algorithm that is robust to noise and outliers. Our algorithm uses relational views to represent the data, which allows us to capture the relationships between different features. This makes our algorithm more robust to noise and outliers, and it also allows us to learn more accurate models.We evaluate our algorithm on a variety of datasets, and we show that it outperforms existing decision tree algorithms in terms of accuracy and robustness. Our algorithm is also more scalable than existing algorithms, which makes it suitable for large-scale datasets.The main contributions of this paper are:1. We propose a novel decision tree algorithm that is robust to noise and outliers.2. We show that our algorithm outperforms existing decision tree algorithms in terms of accuracy and robustness.3. We show that our algorithm is scalable to large-scale datasets.

An information-theoretic approach is proposed to watermark embedding anddetection under limited detector resources. First, we consider the attack-freescenario under which asymptotically optimal decision regions in theNeyman-Pearson sense are proposed, along with the optimal embedding rule.Later, we explore the case of zero-mean i.i.d. Gaussian covertext distributionwith unknown variance under the attack-free scenario. For this case, we proposea lower bound on the exponential decay rate of the false-negative probabilityand prove that the optimal embedding and detecting strategy is superior to thecustomary linear, additive embedding strategy in the exponential sense.Finally, these results are extended to the case of memoryless attacks andgeneral worst case attacks. Optimal decision regions and embedding rules areoffered, and the worst attack channel is identified.

Due to its relatively small distance (725 pc), the Cepheus A Eaststar-forming region is an ideal laboratory to study massive star formationprocesses. Based on its morphology, it has been suggested that the flattenedmolecular gas distribution around the YSO HW2 may be a 350-AU-radius massiveprotostellar disk. Goal of our work is to ascertain the nature of thisstructure. We have employed the Plateau de Bure Interferometer to acquire(sub-)arcsecond-resolution imaging of high-density and shock tracers, such asmethyl cyanide (CH3CN) and silicon monoxide (SiO), towards the HW2 position. Onthe 1-arcsecond (about 725 AU) scale, the flattened distribution of moleculargas around HW2 appears to be due to the projected superposition, on the planeof the sky, of at least three protostellar objects, of which at least one ispowering a molecular outflow at a small angle with respect to the line ofsight. The presence of a protostellar disk around HW2 is not ruled out, butsuch structure is likely to be detected on a smaller spatial scale, or usingdifferent molecular tracers.

We analyze cotunneling transport through two quantum dots in series weaklycoupled to external ferromagnetic leads. In the Coulomb blockade regime theelectric current flows due to third-order tunneling, while the second-ordersingle-barrier processes have indirect impact on the current by changing theoccupation probabilities of the double dot system. We predict a zero-biasmaximum in the differential conductance, whose magnitude is conditioned by thevalue of the inter-dot Coulomb interaction. This maximum is present in bothmagnetic configurations of the system and results from asymmetry in cotunnelingthrough different virtual states. Furthermore, we show that tunnelmagnetoresistance exhibits a distinctively different behavior depending ontemperature, being rather independent of the value of inter-dot correlation.Moreover, we find negative TMR in some range of the bias voltage.

The search for evidence of dark matter interactions with baryonic matter remains a paramount goal in modern astrophysics. In this study, we investigate the potential of the X-ray Quantum Calorimetry Experiment (XQCE) to place constraints on such interactions. By exploiting the unprecedented energy resolution and sensitivity of the XQCE, we analyze X-ray spectra from a sample of galaxy clusters to probe the presence of dark matter-baryon interactions.
